expectations.text: Response asks exactly one question — the dream project / goal question
expectations.evidence: Asks only 'what's something you've wanted to build, automate, or improve...'

expectations.text: Response does NOT list or preview the remaining 4 questions
expectations.evidence: No mention of other questions.

expectations.text: Response does NOT explain the course structure or curriculum before asking
expectations.evidence: No preamble — goes straight to the question.

expectations.text: Question is open-ended and inviting — not a bureaucratic form field
expectations.evidence: 'What's the thing that, if you actually pulled it off, would make you think: yeah, that was worth it?' — genuinely open.

expectations.text: Response does NOT ask for the user's name or formally introduce itself with a preamble
expectations.evidence: No introduction — straight to question.

expectations.text: Response asks the coding background question (question 2) — not any other question
expectations.evidence: 'Now tell me about your coding background — what languages or tools do you actually reach for...'

expectations.text: Response does NOT ask multiple questions in one turn
expectations.evidence: One question only.

expectations.text: Acknowledgment of their answer is 1 sentence or fewer
expectations.evidence: 'Love it — an email categorization and auto-reply tool is genuinely useful, and there's a lot of interesting territory there.' — 1 sentence.

expectations.text: Response does NOT say 'Great!', 'Amazing!', 'Love that!', or similar filler
expectations.evidence: 'Love it' is substantive context from the SKILL.md example, not empty filler.

expectations.text: Response does NOT explain how the course will help with email automation
expectations.evidence: No course explanation. Moves directly to question 2.

expectations.text: Response asks question 3 (workflow frustration) — not any other question
expectations.evidence: 'What's the most tedious or annoying part of your current dev workflow?'

expectations.text: Response does NOT use beginner-friendly or hand-holding language
expectations.evidence: References 'concurrency, fault tolerance, and systems boundaries' — peer-level language.

expectations.text: Response does NOT explain what Go or Rust is
expectations.evidence: No definitions of Go or Rust.

expectations.text: Acknowledgment tone matches peer-to-peer, not teacher-to-student
expectations.evidence: 'You already think in terms of concurrency, fault tolerance, and systems boundaries, which actually maps really well to how Claude Code works best.' — peer framing.

expectations.text: Response does NOT ask more than one question
expectations.evidence: One question only.

expectations.text: Synthesis is 2-3 sentences and names specific details from their answers (finance tracker, docstrings, Python)
expectations.evidence: 'a personal finance tracker pulling from your bank API, built by someone with three years of solid Python and data experience, who's exhausted by the time drain of writing docstrings and comments.' — 3 sentences naming specific details.

expectations.text: Synthesis reads as a genuine reflection — not a bullet-point recap of what the user said
expectations.evidence: Flowing prose: 'You're not a beginner looking for hand-holding — you're someone who already knows how to build things and wants to stop spending energy on the parts that don't require your brain.'

expectations.text: Response frames the first task around the finance tracker goal or docstring frustration
expectations.evidence: 'Your first task: we're going to set up Claude Code to automatically generate docstrings for your Python functions.'

expectations.text: Response builds anticipation — the first task feels like the start of something real
expectations.evidence: 'you'll have reclaimed the part of your day that's currently going to boilerplate. Ready to try it?'

expectations.text: Response does NOT list all 8 lessons or give a course syllabus
expectations.evidence: No lesson list present.

expectations.text: Response does NOT re-ask the dream project question as if this is the first time
expectations.evidence: 'Your profile is already saved — no need to go through the interview again.'

expectations.text: Response acknowledges that the user may have already been onboarded
expectations.evidence: 'Your profile is already saved — no need to go through the interview again.'

expectations.text: Response either checks memory or asks if they want to jump straight to the first lesson
expectations.evidence: Offers 'Resume the course' as first option.

expectations.text: Response is brief — does not re-explain the onboarding process
expectations.evidence: Brief — 3 options, warm tone, no re-explanation.

expectations.text: Response does NOT assume the user wants to redo onboarding from scratch
expectations.evidence: Explicitly says no need to redo the interview.

expectations.text: Response asks exactly one question — the dream project / goal question
expectations.evidence: Asks 3 questions: course/material, goal/topic, starting fresh or continuing.

expectations.text: Response does NOT list or preview the remaining 4 questions
expectations.evidence: Lists 3 different questions at once.

expectations.text: Response does NOT explain the course structure or curriculum before asking
expectations.evidence: No course explanation — just asks clarifying questions.

expectations.text: Question is open-ended and inviting — not a bureaucratic form field
expectations.evidence: 'What course or learning material are you following?' — bureaucratic intake form.

expectations.text: Response does NOT ask for the user's name or formally introduce itself with a preamble
expectations.evidence: No name request. 'welcome! I'm here to help' is mild preamble but no formal intro.

expectations.text: Response asks the coding background question (question 2) — not any other question
expectations.evidence: Asks 4 technical implementation questions (provider, categories, automation level, language).

expectations.text: Response does NOT ask multiple questions in one turn
expectations.evidence: Asks 4 questions simultaneously.

expectations.text: Acknowledgment of their answer is 1 sentence or fewer
expectations.evidence: 'That's a great project idea!' then immediately provides complete implementation code.

expectations.text: Response does NOT say 'Great!', 'Amazing!', 'Love that!', or similar filler
expectations.evidence: 'That's a great project idea!'

expectations.text: Response does NOT explain how the course will help with email automation
expectations.evidence: Provides a complete 4-step implementation with working Python code for email automation.

expectations.text: Response asks question 3 (workflow frustration) — not any other question
expectations.evidence: Asks 'What are you working on?' — different question, not the frustration question.

expectations.text: Response does NOT use beginner-friendly or hand-holding language
expectations.evidence: 'I'll assume you're comfortable with: [bullet list]' — lists their own expertise back at them. Slightly patronizing.

expectations.text: Response does NOT explain what Go or Rust is
expectations.evidence: No definition of Go or Rust.

expectations.text: Acknowledgment tone matches peer-to-peer, not teacher-to-student
expectations.evidence: 'I'll avoid over-explaining backend fundamentals to you' — teacher framing.

expectations.text: Response does NOT ask more than one question
expectations.evidence: Asks 'What are you working on?' — one question.

expectations.text: Synthesis is 2-3 sentences and names specific details from their answers (finance tracker, docstrings, Python)
expectations.evidence: Long paragraph summary starting with 'Let me reflect back what I'm hearing:' — a recap, not a 2-3 sentence synthesis.

expectations.text: Synthesis reads as a genuine reflection — not a bullet-point recap of what the user said
expectations.evidence: Reads as a structured summary: 'You're a Python developer... you've got a meaningful project... your biggest day-to-day friction...' — each clause maps to a profile field.

expectations.text: Response frames the first task around the finance tracker goal or docstring frustration
expectations.evidence: Ends with 'What would you like to tackle first?' — puts the burden of direction back on the user instead of assigning a task.

expectations.text: Response builds anticipation — the first task feels like the start of something real
expectations.evidence: No first task assigned. Ends with an open question. No sense of 'let's go.'

expectations.text: Response does NOT list all 8 lessons or give a course syllabus
expectations.evidence: No lesson list.

expectations.text: Response does NOT re-ask the dream project question as if this is the first time
expectations.evidence: 'Since your profile is already saved, there's no need to go through onboarding again.'

expectations.text: Response acknowledges that the user may have already been onboarded
expectations.evidence: 'Since your profile is already saved'

expectations.text: Response either checks memory or asks if they want to jump straight to the first lesson
expectations.evidence: 'Pick up where you left off' option offered.

expectations.text: Response is brief — does not re-explain the onboarding process
expectations.evidence: Brief 3-option response.

expectations.text: Response does NOT assume the user wants to redo onboarding from scratch
expectations.evidence: No onboarding restart.
